parent.label: The Web
parent.description: Web crawl data is the dominant ingredient in most large language models — CommonCrawl alone processes roughly 3 billion URLs per month. This category covers everything publicly indexable: news sites, personal blogs, corporate pages, how-to guides, recipe sites, opinion columns, and the vast middle of the internet that is neither expertly written nor entirely disposable.
bullets: CommonCrawl: petabyte-scale monthly web snapshots going back to 2008
Quality filters (C4, Gopher, OSCAR) discard ~90% of raw crawl data as spam, boilerplate, or near-duplicate
Web text introduces enormous vocabulary diversity but also misinformation, SEO gibberish, and temporal bias

cells.label: Web Crawls
cells.description: Raw CommonCrawl and derived filtered datasets (C4, Gopher, RefinedWeb) — the backbone of almost every large language model. Hundreds of billions of documents spanning the entire accessible internet.
bullets: C4 (Colossal Cleaned Crawl Corpus): 156GB of filtered English web text
RefinedWeb (Falcon): 5T tokens from CommonCrawl after aggressive deduplication
Dolma's dolmino-mix-1124 allocates 81% of its tokens to web sources

cells.label: News Articles
cells.description: RealNews, CC-News, and other curated news collections give models structured, factual prose: datelines, bylines, the inverted pyramid, and consistent temporal grounding. News writing is dense with proper nouns, organizations, places, and dates.
bullets: RealNews (Grover): ~32GB filtered from CommonCrawl, prioritizing credible news outlets
AP, Reuters, BBC: consistently crawled sources with high factual density
Creates temporal bias — events before the training cutoff are systematically over-represented
cells.screenshotCaption: AP News — one of the most-indexed wire services in training corpora

cells.label: Blogs & Personal Sites
cells.description: Opinionated, personal, and niche writing — the long tail of the web. Technical blogs, personal essays, hobbyist sites, tutorial posts. Less formal than news, more focused than social media, and uniquely rich in first-person voice.
bullets: Technical writers like Simon Willison explain how systems work in meticulous, searchable prose
Substack and Ghost have added millions of newsletter-style essays since 2020
Captures informal registers, domain slang, and the 'I figured this out' voice
cells.screenshotCaption: Simon Willison's weblog — canonical high-quality technical writing
